text: Coiled sleeve, conversion to Roux-en-Y gastric bypass. Patient is a fifty-year-old female with history of hypertension and obstructive sleep apnea. She underwent a laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy two years ago. She did well after the surgery, tolerating diet normally.

Six months ago, she began to experience dysphagia to solids, tolerating only some liquids with constant daily vomiting. Her BMI before sleeve was fifty-four. Her actual BMI prior to the conversion is nineteen.

The upper GI fluoroscopy showed evident passage of contrast with two acute kinks on the sleeve, one superior and another inferior, with a proximal dilatation, as it can be clearly seen in the following images.

The esophagogastroduodenoscopy reveals a large proximal gastric dilatation with an acute angulation that is easily transversed. At the incisura, another acute angulation prevents the passage of the scope despite multiple attempts.

The surgeon places trocars in the abdominal wall for laparoscopic access. During diagnostic laparoscopy, two angulations in the gastric tube are observed, along with adhesions that keep the tube in a fixed position.
The surgeon begins dissection at the hiatus using a monopolar hook, carefully lysing adhesions to free the gastric tube from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the stomach and dissects a posterior window around the gastric tube. A two-stapler load is used to transect the stomach, visibly separating it into distinct sections.
The surgeon retracts the transverse colon to identify the angle of Treitz. The jejunum is measured fifty centimeters from this anatomical landmark and transected using a surgical stapler.
The surgeon measures and runs the elementary limb to a length of 120 centimeters. Enterotomies are created using a monopolar hook, with precise incisions made into the intestinal tissue.
The surgeon performs a side-to-side anastomosis using a stapler, followed by oversewing the enterotomies in two layers with a 3-0 absorbable suture.

The surgeon begins the gastrojejunostomy by suturing a continuous posterior outer layer using 3-0 absorbable sutures. Enterotomies are created with a monopolar hook. A continuous inner-posterior layer is then sutured, followed by an inner-anterior layer. Another suture is started from the opposite side and joined in the middle for reconstruction. The outer anterior layer is completed to finalize the anastomosis.
The OR time was a hundred and twenty minutes. The estimated blood loss, ten cc's. No complications were registered and discharge was post-op day number one.
